title: Quantum vs. Classical Spin: A Comparative Study of Dipolar Spin Dynamics and the Onset of Chaos
authors: Victor Henner, Alexander Nepomnyashchy, Tatyana Belozerova
abstract: We investigate the spin dynamics of a dipole-coupled system by comparing a direct solution of the Schrodinger equation for quantum spins with simulations of classical spins. Although classical spins have long been used in microscopic spin dynamics simulations, we demonstrate that their results differ significantly from those of quantum spins. Using Free Induction Decay as a benchmark, we find that while the overall patterns are qualitatively similar, significant discrepancies emerge at both short and long timescales. We trace these differences to fundamental distinctions in the two descriptions.
summary: This paper compares quantum and classical simulations of dipole-coupled spin systems. It demonstrates that while classical models can approximate quantum dynamics for short periods, they fail at longer timescales because classical nonlinear dynamics lead to chaos and continuous spectra. The authors use Lyapunov exponents to show that classical systems become chaotic for more than three spins, whereas quantum systems remain regular. This research highlights the fundamental limitations of using classical spin-dynamics simulations to represent quantum many-body systems.
main_problem: Investigating the discrepancies between quantum and classical descriptions of spin dynamics in dipole-coupled systems, specifically regarding the onset of chaos and long-term fidelity.
main_result: While quantum and classical simulations show qualitative agreement at short timescales, they diverge significantly at long timescales due to the nonlinearity of classical dynamics, which leads to chaos and a continuous frequency spectrum.
method: The study compares exact diagonalization of the Schrödinger equation for quantum spins with numerical integration of nonlinear gyration equations for classical spins, using Lyapunov exponents to quantify chaos.
model_or_system: A system of N interacting spins (spin-1/2 for quantum, magnetic vectors for classical) in a regular lattice, governed by a Hamiltonian containing a Zeeman term and a dipole-dipole interaction term.
key_observables: Free Induction Decay (FID) of transverse magnetization, Fourier transform of the FID (frequency spectrum), and the maximum Lyapunov exponent.
important_parameters: Dimensionless dipole interaction parameter (dp), number of spins (N), and the characteristic dipole time (t*).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be a rigid lattice, spin-lattice relaxation is neglected, and the classical model relies on a mean-field approximation.
figures_summary: Figures show the time evolution and Fourier transforms of FID for various N, comparisons with Abragam's trial function, the divergence of classical trajectories signaling chaos, and the scaling of the Lyapunov exponent.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantum-classical spin discrepancies, details the quantum and classical models, presents results on FID and spectral features, analyzes the onset of chaos via Lyapunov exponents, and provides an analytical study of the two-spin case in an appendix.
why_interesting: It provides critical insights into the validity of using classical approximations for many-body spin dynamics and highlights how nonlinearity-induced chaos in classical models can obscure quantum spectral features.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
